does: This paper demonstrates a novel jailbreak attack against aligned large language models that exploits natural language registers under-represented in safety training. The authors develop a systematic attack family using real fanfiction subgenres from Archive of Our Own as universal attack carriers, where harmful requests are embedded as narrative climaxes within creative-writing prompts. The method requires no attacker LLM or per-target tuning and achieves substantial increases in attack success rates across eight aligned models, raising mean ASR from 0.278 to 0.731. Through factorial decomposition, the authors show that the attack's effectiveness comes primarily from register mismatch rather than prompt length or structural features, revealing that safety training gaps center on entire categories of natural human writing rather than discrete prompt patterns.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research examining fundamental limitations in LLM alignment and safety training. It extends prior findings that current jailbreaks are easily fingerprinted and patchable by arguing for a deeper problem: under-coverage of natural human linguistic registers in safety data. The paper connects to earlier work on prompt injection, adversarial prompting against aligned models, and benchmark-based evaluation of safety (HarmBench, JailbreakBench). It also responds to emerging insights about distributional misalignment in safety training, where models learn surface-level refusals without understanding the full spectrum of human communication styles. The fanfiction register choice grounds the attack in authentic language variation rather than artificial adversarial constructions.
enables: This research suggests that effective alignment requires not just patching individual exploits but achieving robust safety training across diverse natural language registers and writing styles. It opens directions for future work on characterizing which human communication registers remain under-covered in safety data and developing safety training that generalizes across vernacular variation. The findings indicate that register-based defenses may be fundamentally more robust than template-matching approaches, pointing toward new defense strategies. The work also enables systematic investigation of whether alignment failures correlate with specific linguistic distributions, potentially motivating dataset curation approaches that balance safety coverage with natural language diversity rather than attempting to block adversarial patterns.
